nodes.title: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)
nodes.summary: Uses high-quality examples to move a base model into a controllable, stable, and useful capability regime.
nodes.description: SFT is one of the most fundamental and critical steps in modern LLM training pipelines. It determines whether a model can reliably follow instructions, format outputs, perform basic reasoning, and transfer across tasks, while also serving as the starting point for preference alignment, reinforcement learning, and tool use.

nodes.title: Reinforcement Learning and Post-Training Optimization
nodes.summary: Improves complex reasoning, decision making, and long-horizon performance through rewards, feedback, and online optimization.
nodes.description: In logical-mathematical intelligence, reinforcement learning is not only about classical control. It is also a key method for improving reasoning, planning, and policy quality during post-training. Core concerns include reward design, sample efficiency, exploration strategy, and how to combine offline preference data with online interaction.

nodes.title: Preference Alignment and Reward Modeling
nodes.summary: Explicitly models preference signals for better answers and connects SFT with RL.
nodes.description: Preference alignment studies how human or system preferences can be turned into trainable optimization objectives. It includes reward-model-based approaches such as RLHF as well as direct preference optimization methods such as DPO, making it a core theoretical module for controllability, reasoning quality, and output style.

nodes.title: Inference-Time Expansion and Search
nodes.summary: Brings search, sampling, tree structures, and test-time compute budgets into the reasoning process.
nodes.description: Logical and mathematical tasks often cannot be solved with a single forward pass. They require candidate expansion, search, backtracking, verification, and selection during inference. This direction studies test-time scaling, tree search, program search, and proof search, emphasizing the use of extra compute to obtain more reliable complex reasoning.

nodes.title: AI Infrastructure
nodes.summary: Training, storage, scheduling, parallelism, and cost control form the hard foundation of large-model systems.
nodes.description: AI infrastructure covers the full engineering stack from training to deployment, including cluster scheduling, parallel strategies, memory and bandwidth management, fault tolerance, observability, and cost optimization. Without this layer, many theoretical methods and applications cannot scale reliably to real-world size.

nodes.title: AI for Math
nodes.summary: Application direction for mathematical competitions, proving, conjectures, and formal problem solving.
nodes.description: AI4Math studies how models handle highly structured and highly verifiable mathematical tasks, including geometry problem solving, algebraic derivation, theorem proving, formal mathematics, and research assistance. It is an important application area for testing whether logical reasoning is truly usable.

nodes.title: AI for Science
nodes.summary: Applies model capabilities to scientific problems in proteins, materials, climate, biology, and beyond.
nodes.description: AI4Science is not about generic chat. It is about making models participate in prediction, discovery, simulation, and design for real scientific tasks. For logical-mathematical intelligence, it represents a key path from abstract benchmarks to real scientific problem solving.

nodes.title: Automated Theorem Proving Systems
nodes.summary: Combines proof search, formal systems, and language models into executable systems.
nodes.description: Automated theorem proving systems emphasize end-to-end capability: statement representation, search strategies, tactic generation, verifier calls, and result recovery. They are closer to real systems engineering than isolated papers and are among the most direct and representative application scenarios for logical-mathematical intelligence.

nodes.title: Algorithm Discovery and Program Optimization
nodes.summary: Lets models participate in algorithm search, program rewriting, and computational performance optimization.
nodes.description: These applications apply logical-mathematical intelligence to search and optimization in algorithm space and program space, including matrix multiplication strategy discovery, sorting and compilation optimization, and heuristic search improvement. They show the practical value of models on discrete structures and optimization objectives.

nodes.title: vLLM and Inference Serving
nodes.summary: Builds LLM inference engines around throughput, latency, and memory efficiency.
nodes.description: vLLM represents a key direction in modern inference infrastructure. Through efficient KV cache management, continuous batching, and serving abstractions, it turns large models from research prototypes into deployable online services.

nodes.title: Data and Post-Training Pipelines
nodes.summary: Creates repeatable workflows from data construction and cleaning to preference sampling and multi-stage post-training.
nodes.description: Real post-training capability comes from stable data and workflows, not from one-off experiments. This direction focuses on data generation, data cleaning, synthetic preferences, sample recipes, training orchestration, and engineering implementations of multi-stage SFT and RL alignment pipelines.

nodes.title: Evaluation and Benchmark Infrastructure
nodes.summary: Uses unified benchmarks, scoring protocols, and experiment platforms to measure complex reasoning.
nodes.description: Progress on logical and mathematical tasks depends heavily on trustworthy evaluation. This direction studies benchmark design, automatic evaluation, reproducible experiments, capability profiling, and how to turn open-ended reasoning problems into comparable systems evaluation.

nodes.title: Transformer Architecture
nodes.summary: The foundational architecture behind modern large language models.
nodes.description: The Transformer architecture revolutionized sequence modeling with self-attention and became the dominant architecture in NLP, computer vision, and related fields. Research topics include efficiency optimization, long-context handling, and architectural variants.

nodes.title: Vision Transformer
nodes.summary: Applies Transformer architectures to computer vision.
nodes.description: Vision Transformer (ViT) successfully brought Transformers into computer vision by splitting images into patches and processing them as sequences, reaching or surpassing CNN-level performance.

nodes.title: Diffusion Models
nodes.summary: The dominant approach for generative image modeling.
nodes.description: Diffusion models generate high-quality images through iterative denoising and have become the mainstream technology for image generation. Research includes faster sampling, conditional generation, and video extensions.

nodes.title: Reinforcement Learning Fundamentals
nodes.summary: Learning through interaction between an agent and its environment.
nodes.description: Reinforcement learning studies how agents optimize behavior through reward feedback from interaction with an environment. Core challenges include sample efficiency, exploration-exploitation balance, and generalization.

nodes.title: Speech Synthesis
nodes.description: Speech synthesis has evolved from traditional concatenative systems to end-to-end neural generation, enabling highly natural and expressive speech output. Research focuses on voice cloning, emotional expression, and real-time generation.

nodes.title: Multi-Agent Systems
nodes.summary: Collaboration and competition among multiple AI agents.
nodes.description: Multi-agent systems study how multiple autonomous agents interact, cooperate, or compete in shared environments. Applications include game AI, distributed problem solving, and social simulation.

nodes.title: Meta-Learning
nodes.description: Meta-learning aims to equip AI systems with the ability to learn how to learn, enabling rapid adaptation to new tasks and few-shot settings. Research includes MAML, meta-RL, and automated optimization.

layers.name: Theory Layer
layers.description: Training paradigms, optimization mechanisms, and reasoning methods

layers.name: Application Layer
layers.description: Practical scenarios such as AI4Math, AI4Science, and algorithm discovery

layers.name: Infrastructure Layer
layers.description: Serving, training systems, data engineering, and evaluation platforms
